author: @TreeDancingCloud
text:
This is a fascinating experiment.

At least we can ignore the shape of the lead wires. Why? Because all of the flux passing through the flat surface of one of the flat magnets adds up to the same amount of flux that moves out into the space in the room, hitting the lead wires.The voltage induced radially across the conducting disk adds up. Its sum equals the sum of the voltage increments in the rest of the current loop that includes the test leads, by Kirchhoff's voltage law.

This means we only need to know the rotation speed, field strength, and radius of that conducting disk to calculate the induced voltage. A segment of the loop current flows radially between the outer peripheral contact point and the center axis, and it flows exactly perpendicularly to the flux that goes into one flat face of the magnet, and out of the other flat magnet face. The voltage across that one clearly defined segment of the current flow path equals the voltage across all the rest of the current loop, including test leads and all.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
One common mistake made by many is assuming that if a magnet spins, its magnetic field also  rotates.   but when the magnet spins on an axis through its poles.  the magnetic field remains static and unchanging.  the homopolar generator works with and without the magnets spinning about its polar axis. 

Thus if an electron moves tangently to that magnetic field, it feels a force pushing it tangently to both the magnetic field and the direction of the electron's motion.  Protons feel a force in the other direction but the protons in metal atoms are locked in crystal lattice, thus only electrons in the conduction band are free to move if a circuit path is available, and no counter electric field is seen by the electron.  that's why current collectors must be stationary while the faraday disk is rotating. Otherwise a circuit path in rotation creates an equal and reverse electric field preventing current from flowing.

author: @billallen275
text: I LOVE taking things apart for "useful bits"! Been doing it since I was a kid... Played with these a few years ago. I've read that these have been used in industry to generate thousands of Amperes at low voltage (now obsolete). The brushes are a major problem, and mercury has been used as a conductor with all sorts of other issues, as you could imagine.  There is only 1 turn in the generator here. Like a single turn through a toroidal core. Your probe/contact is stationary whilst the disk assembly spins underneath. I think that is the place where the lines intersect the wire with relative motion. The field goes from the outside surface of one magnet to the other one, with your conductor sandwiched between.   I propose that a "coaxial" arrangement picked up at the axles will not produce significant induced voltage. Still, these are fascinating.  BTW, this is, IMO, the same as the "tether" experiment with the Space Shuttle, where the tether exploded from induced current.  Cheers!

author: @gcewing
text:
(EDIT: I've had second thoughts about this -- see my reply.)

Coincidentally I've just been reading Feynman's lectures on electrodynamics, and I think I can answer this. You're mistaken in thinking that the emf is due to relative motion between the wire and the magnet. All that matters is whether there is a magnetic field in the region of space through which the wire is moving, in the frame of reference where the motion is taking place. Spinning the magnet does not make the magnetic field disappear. (If it did, spinning a pair of magnets stuck to each other would make them fall apart, which it clearly doesn't.)

As for the eddy currents, I wouldn't expect there to be any when the field is uniform and covering the whole disk, because all the emfs are radial and there is no return path for the currents. I haven't seen your homopolar generator video yet, but i'm guessing it has poles that only cover part of the disk. In that case, there are return paths through other parts of the disk where the field and therefore the emf is weaker.

I think if you shorted the output of this version of the generator you would get some drag due to eddy currents, although it might not be noticeable unless you can make a really low resistance connection.

replies.author: @gcewing
replies.text:
Having thought about it some more, I've changed my mind! I now think there is *no* emf induced in the disk itself. Here's why: In a  reference frame that moves along with a piece of the disk at some moment in time (let's call this the "conductor frame"),  there is no reason to expect any emf, because in that frame you just have a stationary conductor and a stationary magnet. So there can't be any emf in the disk in the laboratory frame either. It's true that the conductor is moving in a magnetic field in that frame, which produces an emf; *but* the transformation of the magnetic field from the conductor frame to the laboratory frame produces an *electric* field that cancels it out!

So where does the voltage measured by the meter come from? I think it's coming from the *rest* of the circuit, which is also passing through the field from the magnets. In the laboratory frame, it's because of the electric field produced by the moving magnets. In the conductor frame, it's because the rest of the circult is moving through the magnetic field in the opposite direction.

Electrodynamics is tricky stuff. There's a lot going on, and you have to take all of it into account to get a consistent picture.

Electrons moving at 90 degrees to a magnetic field are pushed at 90 degrees to both the direction motion and the magnetic field.  So electons in the conduction band of a spinning conductor are pushed to one side when moved through a stationary magnetic field. That force is the electro motive force, voltage. When you complet a circuit with a wire external to much of the magnetic field, current flows. 


If you  make a loop in a wire and run current through that wire you create an electro magnet.  If you rotate that coil about its polar axis,  the coil is rotated but the magnetic field does not change.  In other words, you can rotate a magnet on its pole but you can not rotate a magnetic field about the pole, thus it matters not if you spin the magnet about its pole. The field is stationary either way.

author: @chipjarred
text: Have you tried it with no magnet attached as a control?  It occurs to me that the aluminum disk does have relative motion with a magnetic field... the Earth's.   Trying it with different orientations would be enlightening, since Maxwell's equations predict that the current is proportional to the velocity perpendicular to the magnetic field (ie, the axis of rotation is parallel to the magnetic field).  If the Earth's magnetic field is involved, you'd expect the greatest output with the axis of rotation oriented north-south, and a minimum if the axis is in the east-west/up-down plane.   As someone else pointed out, there is relative motion between the plate and the wires/brushes.  I have read (but not verified) that homopolar generator experiments have found that rotating the magnet while keeping the disk stationary does not  produce current, but that assumes the field is uniform and the rotation sufficiently smooth.  In practice it may vary somewhat due to vibration and the fact that the magnetization of a permanent magnet is rarely as uniform as one might wish, so the field experienced by the attaching wires might vary slightly as the magnets rotate.  But that relative motion with the brushes and wires means there's also friction, so it could also just be a triboelectric effect.  Trying it with no magnets and in different orientations would test these hypotheses.  If its current is the same or similar with no magnets and in all orientations, then it's consistent with the triboelectric effect.  If you get different currents depending on orientation, that would be consistent with an external magnetic field, most likely the Earth's if the maximum is in the north-south orientation,  though there could be another nearby magnetic source you're unaware of that could explain it if the maximum is another orientation (easily tested for with a compass and some prior knowledge of which way is north).

replies.author: @chipjarred
replies.text:
As a quick explanatory note, the modern Heaviside formulation of Maxwell's equations doesn't include the relevant term to predict the behavior.  You have to include Lorentz force law.   However, in both Maxwell's original paper for the Royal Society, "A dynamical theory of electricity", and in his later treatise, in his equations that define the electric strength field, E,  in terms of the scalar and vector potential fields, he includes a term relating it to velocity relative to a magnetic flux field, B.  That term is equivalent to the B term in the Lorentz force equation, so the modern formulation is essentially the same.  Bits of the original have just been offloaded to other equations. 

It's interesting to read the original papers to see how the form of the equations has changed.  In the original paper, he writes them down as 20 individual component equations (so 3 each for Ampere's Law and Faraday's Law, and 1 for each of Gauss's Laws, plus a few others no longer directly included under the name "Maxwell's equations"), but by the time he wrote his treatise, he had become enamored with Hamilton's quaternions, so he uses that notation, which condenses them closer to what we have today, though he doesn't do any actual quaternion math with them.   Oliver Heaviside simplified them a bit more with his own notation, moving current density components directly into Ampere's law, eliminated the continuity equation, as it can be derived from the others, etc...

I think your conclusion is it is generating power on its own but you have to consider the fact you are in a lab surrounded by RF generated by AC transmission in your household electrical system. (proven by touching the open input of any audio anplifer with your finger) you are acting as an antenna.
What you might be observing is something being transfered by RF. A test would be taking this all outdoors away from buildings and electrical lines. and not holding carbon with your hand. (drills might also generate an RF as they spark the comutator)
Also there might be a phenomenon of carbon against the wheel that is generating the numbers you see..
try it without the magnets as a control. (update,,ok  I see that you did that) if you are getting that spike when you touch carbon to just the metal spinning you might be forming some sort of temporary battery or capacitor that gets charged by the RF in the room.
Then spinning the metal is like continually touching and untouching the carbon to the metal.
but if this only happened with magnets then some magnetic field might be cutting across test leads.
or is the magnetic field actually lagging by some inertia effect?
I am not a physicist I just play one on the interwebs.

author: @alexwild4350
text:
Hi Robert, I wish I could remember what it was called. There was a wheel, rather large, like 8 foot diameter, made of Iron ? I can't recall. Anyway, when spun with a brush on the outer rim, perhaps the other connection taken from the spindle, it produced whopping great currents. In the order of thousands of amps? at a couple of volts. The spinning wheel did find application in Welding. Only a couple were ever made for specific jobs. Part of the problem is that even the tiniest contact resistance on the rim of the wheel, because of the thousands of amps produced, created serious heat issues on the brush assembly and melted it in the blink of an eye, the rest of the current being used for the welding process.
Overall I think the sheer size of the wheel, prorbably weight as well, and inability to move it around, ended up favouring the welding systems we all know and love. I just can't remember the name given to the system. So its not a new discovery. Industry has used this effect.

If you hold the view that the field flux is a physical entity, it does rotate or depends on how it is generated. author: @supercoe
text: Robert, when I played with this effect it opened my mind to the idea that a magnetic field is stationary on it's axis. I believe rotating a magnet like you did is not rotating the field, therefore the relationship between motion and power is realized when you imagine the conductor moving through a stationary field. As you've found, the lack of eddy currents with this style unipolar generator under no load makes it interesting. Thanks for the detailed video as always!

replies.author: @overunity_inventor
replies.text: When you rotate a magnet with copper plate such that they have zero relative motion, then magnets magnetic field rotate with the magnet and interacts with the earth magnetic field lines which are present everywhere on earth, that's why a compass always points to North & South. That relative motion between the earth magnetic field and magnets magnetic field generates the current in the copper plate.

author: @michaelinamoment
text:
I think it's of the characteristic of the fields. We typically think of them as being fixed fields around our objects. If there was any sort of lag at all it's less than the speed of light then what you're maybe seeing is a small oscillation of the field as it moves in space. Even if you have a wire moving relative with it the field itself is moving in a lag behind the objects.  Or at least it's my thoughts on why you're detecting a small amount of current. 
The reason for the leg I imagine may be the fact that you charge particles in the air as a repercussion of there being a field. That air dust particles or whatever maybe a secondary field source causing this lag of the field.
